data:
Search results for "PLY Scheme parser example token list AST nested":

Title: An Introduction to Scheme and its Implementation - The Reader
URL: https://docs.scheme.org/schintro/schintro_115.html
Content: A token is just a fairly simple item that doesn't have a nested structure. For example, lists nest, but symbol names don't, strings don't, and numbers don't.

Title: Abstract Syntax Tree - powershell.one
URL: https://powershell.one/powershell-internals/parsing-and-tokenization/abstract-syntax-tree
Content: The Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) groups tokens into meaningful structures and is the most sophisticated way of analyzing PowerShell code. These tokens are used for example by editors to colorize the code and show variables in a different color than commands. > *Abstract Syntax Tree* is called *tree* because it works like a hierarchical tree: **PowerShell** starts with the first token and then takes the **PowerShell** language definition (*syntax*) to see what the next possible tokens could be. > According to the **PowerShell** language definition, a *string* token can only be followed by an *operator* token which is why this line is syntactically correct, and when you execute it, **PowerShell** knows what to do and emits an excited greeting:. * **ScriptBlock:** a *scriptblock* is a valid chunk of **PowerShell** code, so it has already been processed by the parser, and the parser has guaranteed that there are no syntax errors in the code.

Title: Writing Your First Compiler - Part 4: Abstract Syntax Trees & Recursive Descent
URL: https://dev.to/frederikgramkortegaard/writing-your-first-compiler-part-4-abstract-syntax-trees-recursive-descent-3j41
Content: # Writing Your First Compiler - Part 4: Abstract Syntax Trees & Recursive Descent - DEV Community. # Writing Your First Compiler - Part 4: Abstract Syntax Trees & Recursive Descent. It takes `(2 + 3) * 4` and gives us a neat list of tokens: `[LParen, Number(2), Plus, Number(3), RParen, Mul, Number(4), Eof]`. When you see `+`, does it happen before or after the multiplication? The tokens are just sitting there in a line - no indication of what connects to what. We need to capture that structure. We need to know that `*` operates on `(2 + 3)` and `4`. We need to know that the parentheses group things, that operations have operands, that some things happen before others. This structure is hierarchical - it's a tree, not a list. That's why we use trees. Each node is a piece of your program - a number, an operation, a function call. Let's look at a simple expression: `2 + 3`.

Title: How to build an AST from Tokens? : r/Compilers - Reddit
URL: https://www.reddit.com/r/Compilers/comments/hk69qn/how_to_build_an_ast_from_tokens
Content: This chapter explains what the trees should look like and the next chapter walks you through writing a parser that takes a series of tokens and creates an AST

Title: Building an AST Parser Pt 1: Scanning
URL: https://pasanpremaratne.com/2018/08/31/building-ast-parser-part-1
Content: When we run or compile a text file containing source code there are a set of steps that take us to the machine code or bytecode that is eventually executed. In the scanning step, which is what we’ll focus on here, we’re going to scan the source code and generate a series of tokens. For each token we’re going to indicate what type of token it is and since there’s a limited set defined in Swift’s lexical grammar we can use an enum. We also need to generate a token when we reach the end of the source code and for that we’ll define an end of file token type, `eof`. Token is fairly simple for now and defines the type of the token and the line number in the source file on which the lexeme was defined. Once we have a token the next step is to define a lexer or a scanner that scans each character of the source code and generates a set of tokens.
